name: Terbium
name_origin: Named after the village of Ytterby in Sweden, source of several rare-earth minerals.

occurrence.abundance_summary: One of the rarer of the rare-earth elements (~1.2 ppm in crust); recovered chiefly from monazite, bastnaesite, and xenotime as a minor component.

compounds.name: Terbium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: White solid that turns brown-black on partial oxidation; precursor for terbium phosphors and metal.

compounds.name: Terbium oxide (mixed valence)
compounds.summary: Dark brown commercial form of terbium oxide used as a green phosphor activator and starting material.

compounds.name: Terbium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: White hygroscopic salt used as a precursor to terbium phosphors and metal.

compounds.name: Terfenol-D
compounds.summary: Giant magnetostrictive alloy that changes length significantly in a magnetic field; used in actuators and sonar transducers.

uses: Green phosphors in trichromatic fluorescent lamps and CRT displays (e.g., LaPO4:Ce,Tb)
Activator in green-emitting X-ray intensifying screens (Gd2O2S:Tb)
Magnetostrictive Terfenol-D actuators, sonars, and acoustic devices
Solid-state device memory and magneto-optical recording media
Stabiliser in high-temperature fuel cells (calcium fluoride and zirconium dioxide)
Dopant in solid-state lasers and as an activator in long-persistence phosphors
reactivity_summary: Silvery-white ductile rare-earth metal; reasonably stable in dry air but tarnishes slowly in moist air. Reacts slowly with cold water, more rapidly with hot water and acids. Two allotropes interconvert near 1250 degrees C.
isotopes_summary: Terbium has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope (Tb-159). Many synthetic radioisotopes are known, of which Tb-158 (half-life 180 years) and Tb-157 (~71 years) are the longest-lived.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Terbium
